task: Explain the self-attention formula and why scaling by sqrt(dk) is important.

steps.input: self-attention formula scaled dot product sqrt dk attention scores
steps.reasoning: This is a technical question about the self-attention formula. I need to retrieve the relevant mathematical explanation from the knowledge base.

tool_result.query: self-attention formula scaled dot product sqrt dk attention scores

results.title: The Self-Attention Mechanism
results.snippet: Self-attention operates on three matrices: Queries (Q), Keys (K), and Values (V). The attention score between positions i and j is the dot product of query_i and key_j, scaled by sqrt(dk) to prevent vanishing gradients. Formally: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(dk)) x V. Multi-head attention runs h parallel attention functions with different learned projections.

results.title: Introduction to Transformer Architecture
results.snippet: The core innovation is the self-attention mechanism, which allows each token to attend to all other tokens simultaneously. Each layer contains two sub-layers: multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network.

results.title: BERT: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers
results.snippet: BERT uses two pre-training objectives: Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP).

steps.input: Explain the self-attention formula and why scaling by sqrt(dk) is important.
steps.reasoning: doc_004 contains the exact formula and the explanation for scaling. I have enough context to answer directly.
steps.tool_result:
The self-attention formula is:

  Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(dk)) × V

Where:
- Q (Queries), K (Keys), V (Values) are linear projections of the input.
- QK^T computes the dot-product similarity between every pair of positions.
- dk is the dimension of the key vectors.

Why divide by sqrt(dk)?
As dk grows larger, dot products can become very large in magnitude, pushing the softmax function into regions of extremely small gradients (near-zero or near-one outputs). Dividing by sqrt(dk) keeps the dot products in a numerically stable range, ensuring the softmax outputs a well-spread distribution of attention weights rather than a near-one-hot distribution. This prevents vanishing gradients during training and helps the model learn meaningful attention patterns.

final_answer: Self-attention formula: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(dk)) × V. Scaling by sqrt(dk) prevents large dot products from pushing softmax into vanishing-gradient regions, ensuring numerically stable and well-distributed attention weights.
